cue: How do you say someone gave it everything they had?

es.text: Poner toda la carne en el asador
es.literal: to put all the meat on the grill
es.meaning: To go all in; to commit everything to one attempt. ADMIRING.
es.example: Puso toda la carne en el asador y produjo la película ella misma.
es.exampleTranslation: She went all in and produced the film herself.

it.literal: to put too much meat on the fire
it.meaning: To take on too much at once; to overcommit. CRITICAL.
it.example: Hai messo troppa carne al fuoco: non riuscirai a finire niente.
it.exampleTranslation: You've taken on too much — you'll finish nothing.

bridge.note: The same meat on the same fire, and the two languages deliver opposite verdicts. Spanish admires it: all the meat on the grill, holding nothing back, the gambler's whole stake. Italian scolds it: TOO much meat on the fire, more than anyone could cook, ambition curdling into chaos. The word that decides the judgement is troppa — and a Spanish speaker who translates their own compliment has just called someone reckless.

interference.es_to_it: The costly one: praising someone with 'ha messo tutta la carne al fuoco' lands as criticism. For genuine all-in praise Italian says 'ha dato tutto se stesso'.
interference.it_to_es: Translating the Italian warning into Spanish produces a compliment — 'poner toda la carne en el asador' is admiring. To warn, Spanish says 'abarcar demasiado'.

review.notes: ES side discovered in the Spanish idioms book Joe is reading, 2026-07-21 (discovery only; all glosses and examples authored fresh). IT counterpart from Claude's recall — UNVERIFIED. The valence flip flagged in CANDIDATES since the Europass harvest — now confirmed from the book. Top-tier entry; the trap has real social cost.
